description: Adversarial inputs for WaterfallType.fromSourceText — P10 Sub-task 5.1

entries.rationale: Spec lists IntType as primitive; base matches "int" branch.
Spec lists DecType as primitive; base matches "dec" branch.
Spec lists BoolType as primitive; base matches "bool" branch.
Spec lists CharType as primitive; base matches "char" branch.
Bare "void" matches the "void" branch; VoidType is constructible per §1.2.
Ends in "[]"; base "int" → IntType; returns ArrayType(IntType).
Ends in "[]"; base "dec" → DecType; returns ArrayType(DecType).
Ends in "[]"; base "bool" → BoolType; returns ArrayType(BoolType).
Ends in "[]"; base "char" → CharType; returns ArrayType(CharType).
PITFALL #1: the void[] guard explicitly returns ErrorType(text) before constructing ArrayType(VoidType).
First check: startsWith("?") returns ErrorType(text) verbatim (audit surprise #5).
Leading "?" short-circuits before the array check; ErrorType holds full original text including the brackets.
startsWith("?") fires even with no following identifier; returns ErrorType("?").
Empty string: no "?" prefix, doesn't end in "[]", base "" doesn't match any primitive → ErrorType("").
Not in {int,dec,bool,char,void}; falls through to else branch → ErrorType(text).
Spec uses lowercase exact match in when-expression; "String" is unknown → ErrorType.
Case-sensitive when match; "Int" ≠ "int" → ErrorType("Int").
Case-sensitive when match; "INT" ≠ "int" → ErrorType("INT").
Kotlin class name leaked from caller; fromSourceText consumes source-language text only, not Kotlin identifiers.
Leading whitespace not stripped by spec; " int" ≠ "int" in when match → ErrorType(" int").
Trailing whitespace not stripped; doesn't end "[]", base "int " ≠ "int" → ErrorType("int ").
Single space: no "?" prefix, no "[]" suffix, base " " not in primitive set → ErrorType(" ").
Ends in "[]" → base = "int[]"; "int[]" is not in {int,dec,bool,char,void} → ErrorType("int[][]"). Nested arrays not supported in P10.
Ends in "[]" → base = ""; empty base not in primitive set → ErrorType("[]").
Doesn't end exactly in "[]" (only single "["); base "int[" ≠ "int" → ErrorType("int[").
Doesn't end in "[]"; base "int]" not in primitive set → ErrorType("int]").
Ends in "[]" → base = "void[]"; "void[]" not in primitive set → ErrorType. (Wouldn't reach the void[] guard since base isn't "void".)
startsWith("?") matches on first char, returns ErrorType verbatim including second "?".
Trailing "?" doesn't trip the prefix check; base "int?" doesn't match any primitive → ErrorType("int?").

Spec says nothing about whitespace normalization. Reading §1.2 literally: the when-expression compares the raw base string against "int" — a leading tab byte makes it unequal. Prediction: ErrorType("\tint"). Alternative interpretation: caller is expected to pre-trim; spec is silent. If the parser ever feeds untrimmed text (e.g., from token whitespace) this is the regression vector. Going with the literal reading.

Doesn't end in "[]" (suffix is "extra"); base = "int[]extra" doesn't match. Prediction: ErrorType. Spec uses endsWith("[]") which is unambiguous — but a careless impl using contains("[]") would mis-parse this. This is the silent-resolution probe for substring-vs-suffix confusion.

Doesn't end in "[]"; base "voidvoid" ≠ any primitive in the when. Prediction: ErrorType. Probes whether the impl might accidentally use startsWith/contains instead of exact equality on base — a confused impl could see "void" prefix and dispatch to VoidType incorrectly.

Very long identifier (>1KB): no "?" prefix, no "[]" suffix, base doesn't match any primitive → ErrorType with full original text preserved. Probes for any silent truncation in error-path code. Per §1.2 ErrorType(text), the sourceText field must equal the input exactly — no shortening even for debug-print convenience.

Ends in "[]" → base = "int " (trailing space); "int " ≠ "int" in when → ErrorType. Spec doesn't normalize whitespace inside the base after stripping suffix. A trim-the-base impl would (incorrectly per the literal spec) accept this as int[]. This is the silent-resolution probe for internal-whitespace handling.

Unicode confusable: 'ì' (U+00EC) not 'i' (U+0069). Kotlin String equality is char-exact, so "ìnt" ≠ "int" → ErrorType. Spec doesn't mention Unicode normalization; predicting straight ErrorType. A future impl that runs the input through Unicode NFKD normalization would still preserve the accent; only a strip-accents pass (which spec doesn't authorize) would resolve it to IntType.
